name: God (Trinity)
shortDescription: In Christian theology, God is understood as a Trinity: Father, Son (Jesus Christ), and Holy Spirit, three distinct persons united in one divine being.
description: The Christian concept of God is fundamentally Trinitarian, meaning God exists as a unity of three distinct persons: the Father, the Son (Jesus Christ), and the Holy Spirit. These three are co-equal, co-eternal, and consubstantial (of one substance). The Father is often understood as the ultimate source of all things, the Son as the Word of God incarnate who reveals God to humanity and redeems humanity from sin, and the Holy Spirit as the power and presence of God actively working in the world and in the lives of believers. The doctrine of the Trinity developed over several centuries, with early church fathers grappling with how to reconcile the monotheistic nature of Judaism with the divinity of Jesus Christ and the evident presence of the Holy Spirit. Key councils, such as the Council of Nicaea (325 AD) and the Council of Constantinople (381 AD), played a crucial role in formulating the Nicene Creed, a foundational statement of Christian belief that affirms the Trinity. The Trinity is not explicitly laid out in the Old Testament but is interpreted as being foreshadowed in various passages. The New Testament provides more explicit indications, particularly in the Gospels and the writings of Paul. The Trinity is central to Christian worship, prayer, and understanding of salvation. Different Christian denominations hold slightly varying interpretations of the specific relationships within the Trinity, but the core belief in one God existing in three persons remains a defining characteristic of Christian faith.
